subUnits.title: 1.1 Numbers 0-69: Review and the Pattern System
notes: - These are all unique words that must be memorized (zéro, un, deux, trois... dix-neuf).
- From 20 onwards, French follows a clear pattern: TENS word + hyphen + UNITS word.
- For 21, 31, 41, 51, and 61, use 'et un' (and one) instead of a hyphen.
- Example: vingt et un (21), trente et un (31).
- Note: The 'et' is ONLY used before 'un', not before any other number!
- Knowing numbers is essential for everyday life in Nigeria, from bargaining in markets like Balogun or Ariaria to giving directions and understanding prices in Naira.
subUnits.youtubeQuery: JSS1 French numbers 0 to 69 pronunciation site:youtube.com

quiz.q: How do you say '17' in French?

quiz.explanation: For 21, 31, 41, 51, 61, we use 'et un' (e.g., vingt et un).

subUnits.title: 1.2 Numbers 70-100: The Tricky Part!
notes: - France uses 60 (soixante) as the base for 70s.
- 71 = soixante et onze (60 + 11). Note the 'et' before 'onze'!
- 81 = quatre-vingt-un (The 's' is dropped when another number follows).

- While Belgium and Switzerland use simpler words (septante, huitante, nonante), standard French (taught in Nigeria) uses the mathematical system of 70, 80, and 90. Mastering this is a major milestone in French fluency!
subUnits.youtubeQuery: JSS1 French numbers 70 to 100 pronunciation site:youtube.com

quiz.q: How do you say '70' in French?

quiz.explanation: 80 is 'quatre-vingts' (4 x 20). It takes an 's' because no number follows.
71 is 'soixante et onze'. The 'et' is used because it ends in 'onze' (11).
81 is 'quatre-vingt-un'. The 's' on 'vingt' is dropped when followed by another number.

subUnits.title: 1.3 Large Numbers and Ordinal Numbers
notes: - 100 = cent. 200 = deux cents (adds 's' only if it ends the number).
- 201 = deux cent un (no 's' on 'cent' because a number follows).
- 1,000 = mille. NOTE: 'mille' NEVER takes an 's', even in plural (e.g., deux mille).
- 1,000,000 = un million (takes 's' in plural: deux millions).
- Formed by adding '-ième' to the cardinal number.
- 1st = premier (masc) / première (fem). This is the ONLY irregular one!
- Spelling changes: 4th = quatrième (drop 'e'), 5th = cinquième (add 'u'), 9th = neuvième ('f' becomes 'v').
- Nigerian phone numbers are read in PAIRS of digits, just like in France! (e.g., 0801 234 5678 is read as 'zéro huit, zéro un, vingt-trois...').
- Ordinal numbers are used for floors in buildings (le premier étage), dates (le premier janvier), and rankings.
diagramDescriptions: Chart of ordinal numbers with spelling changes
subUnits.youtubeQuery: JSS1 French large numbers and ordinal numbers site:youtube.com

quiz.q: How do you say '1,000' in French?

quiz.explanation: 200 is 'deux cents'. 'Cent' takes an 's' when multiplied and not followed by another number.
201 is 'deux cent un'. The 's' is dropped from 'cent' because another number follows.
9th is 'neuvième'. The 'f' in 'neuf' changes to 'v' before '-ième'.

quiz.q: Does the word 'mille' ever take an 's' in the plural?
quiz.explanation: 'Mille' NEVER takes an 's', even in the plural (e.g., deux mille).
4th is 'quatrième'. The silent 'e' in 'quatre' is dropped before adding '-ième'.
